---
title: "Intro to ggplot and Data visualization"
author: "YOUR NAME HERE"
subtitle: ggplot, geom_point
output:
  pdf_document: default
  html_document:
    df_print: paged
urlcolor: blue
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
---

## Learning Goals:

-   identify basics of a dataset: rows, columns, variables
-   gain familiarity with basic R syntax, tidyverse
-   identify a function and its structure (has (), inputs/arguments go inside ())
-   make a scatterplot
-   describe trends shown in a scatterplot

------------------------------------------------------------------------

First, we load the packages we will be using and the data:

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| message: FALSE
library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
data(penguins)

```

# Background

For today's exercise, we will use the `penguins` dataset. Be sure that you have run the chunk above, because "penguins" is from the `palmerpenguins` library.

More details about palmerpenguins [here](https://allisonhorst.github.io/palmerpenguins/)

We can view the dataset by typing its name:

```{r}
penguins

# to see the first 10 rows and all columns
print(penguins, n = 10, width = Inf)   
```

We can view the help file for penguins in this way:

```{r}
?penguins
```

<!-- This documentation describes the variables. ![image from https://allisonhorst.github.io/palmerpenguins/reference/figures/culmen_depth.png](~/Sds_101A_SSI26/Class/Code/Workshop_Day1/Images/images/image_culmen_depth.png) -->

<!-- We will be examining characteristics of three species of penguins: Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo. ![image from https://www.bas.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Penguin-heights.jpg](~/Sds_101A_SSI26/Class/Code/Workshop_Day1/Images/images/image_penguin_heights.jpg) -->

1)  How many rows are in the dataset? What does each row represent?

2)  For each of the following variables, indicate whether it is categorical or numerical. If it is categorical, do the categories have a specific order? If it is numerical, is it discrete (whole numbers) or continuous (decimals possible)?

species

island

bill_length_mm

body_mass_g

year

# Making a scatterplot with geom_point()

To examine the relationship between two numerical variables, we can use a *scatter plot*.

An example plot:

```{r}
ggplot( #LINE 1 
  data = penguins, #LINE 2
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) + #LINE 3
  geom_point() #LINE 4
```

3)  Identify what each line in the above code does:

Line 1:

Line 2:

Line 3:

Line 4:

4)  Read the warning message that appears. What does it mean? Why is important that R notifies you of missing values?

# Mapping aesthetics to variables

5)  Modify the code below to map the color aesthetic to `species` (i.e. each species of penguin should be a different color).

```{r}
ggplot( 
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) + 
  geom_point() 
```

# Setting aesthetics to fixed values

```{r}
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(
      x = flipper_length_mm, 
      y = body_mass_g, 
      color = "blue"
    )
  )

ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(
      x = flipper_length_mm, 
      y = body_mass_g
    ),
    color = "blue"
  )
```

6)  Explain the difference in the two plots above. Be sure to run the plots one at a time by using "Cmd + Enter"

7)  When should an aesthetic be inside aes() and when should it be outside aes()?

# Mapping multiple aesthetics

```{r}
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(
    x = flipper_length_mm, 
    y = body_mass_g, 
    color = species, 
    size= bill_depth_mm)) + 
  geom_point()
```

8)  In the code above, remove `size = bill_depth_mm` and try to instead map shape to species. Add that to the plot above!

9)  What happens if we *also* map size to species? Do you like the look of this plot?

# Shorter code

Note that each of the following produce the exact same graph!!! 

```{r}
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(
    x = flipper_length_mm,
    y = body_mass_g, 
    color = species)) + 
  geom_point()
  
ggplot(
  data = penguins, 
  aes(
    x = flipper_length_mm, 
    y = body_mass_g, 
    color = species)) + 
  geom_point() 

ggplot(
  penguins, 
  aes(
    x = flipper_length_mm, 
    y = body_mass_g, 
    color = species)) + 
  geom_point() 
```

# Aesthetics across layers.

```{r}
ggplot(
  data = penguins, 
  aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method = lm, se = FALSE)
```

11) What does the option `se = FALSE` do? (Remove it and run and see what happens!)

12) What does the option `method = lm` do? (Remove it and run and see what happens!)

13) Be sure to undo both the changes above so that it reads `geom_smooth(method = lm, se = FALSE)` again. Now compare that plot above to this one:

```{r}
ggplot(
  data = penguins, 
  aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) + 
  geom_point(aes(color = species)) + 
  geom_smooth(method = lm, se = FALSE)
```

Describe the change in the graph's appearance. What change in the code led to this change in the plot?

# More practice.

14) Write code to create the plot below. 

<!-- (If you'd like to see it bigger, go to the images folder and click on it.) ![length depth plot](~/Sds_101A_SSI26/Class/Code/Workshop_Day1/Images/images/plot_length_depth_penguins.png) -->

To insert an R chunk here, you can use Ctr+Alt+I, the green chunk button, OR Code \> Insert chunk.

```{r, echo=F, warning=F, message = F}

penguins |> 
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(aes(bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, color = species))+
  geom_smooth(aes(bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, color = species), method = "lm", se = F)

```


# Practice with figuring out errors!

Fixing errors in your code is a useful skill! If you get an error message, first read the entire message to see if it provides clues about what the error is or where it occurred.

Sometimes the messages are pretty cryptic, particularly when you are just learning R. Hopefully, it can give you a sense of at least WHERE the problem is.

If the message doesn't point you in a particular direction, just start at the beginning and carefully compare to example code that you know works properly.

15) For each of the graphs below, explain why we get errors (or they don't work as anticipated). Hint: Copy the code to a new chunk so that you can keep the original code for reference. Run one graph at a time with Ctrl+Enter (PC) or Command+Enter (Mac). Edit the code to make it work.

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# Example 1
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_point(
    aes(
      x = flipper_length_mm, 
      y = body_mass_g, 
      shape = 21, 
      size = 5
    )
  )

# Example 2
ggplot(data = penguins) 
  + geom_point(
      aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g), 
      shape = 21, 
      size = 5
    )

# Example 3
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_point(
    x = flipper_length_mm, 
    y = body_mass_g, 
    shape = 21, 
    size = 5
  )

# Example 4
ggplot(data = penguins) + 
  geom_point(
    aes(
      x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g
    ), 
    color = species, 
    size = 5
  )

# Example 5
ggplot(data = penguins) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = flipper_length, y = body_mass_g))
```
